annotations.label: Ring theory

anchor.snippet: ring theory is the study of rings

annotations.note: A description of a mathematical field, not a formalizable statement, though the object `Ring` exists in Mathlib.Algebra.Ring.Defs.

annotations.label: Commutative ring

anchor.snippet: A ring is called commutative if its multiplication is commutative

annotations.note: `CommRing` is exactly a ring with commutative multiplication.

annotations.label: Integral domain

anchor.snippet: non-trivial commutative rings where no two non-zero elements multiply to give zero

annotations.note: `IsDomain` captures nontrivial no-zero-divisor rings (commutative integral domain = `CommRing` + `IsDomain`), generalized to not require commutativity.

annotations.label: Principal ideal domain

anchor.snippet: Principal ideal domains are integral domains in which every ideal can be generated by a single element

annotations.note: `IsPrincipalIdealRing` (every ideal is principal) combined with `IsDomain` gives a PID; the Mathlib class is more general as it does not require a domain.

annotations.label: Euclidean domain

anchor.snippet: Euclidean domains are integral domains in which the Euclidean algorithm can be carried out

annotations.note: `EuclideanDomain` is a commutative domain equipped with a Euclidean function.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz correspondence

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz that establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the points of an algebraic variety

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the points-to-maximal-ideals correspondence (and `vanishingIdeal_zeroLocus_eq_radical`) over an algebraically closed field.

annotations.label: Spectrum of a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: the spectrum of a commutative ring is the space of its prime ideals equipped with Zariski topology

annotations.note: `PrimeSpectrum` is the set of prime ideals, with `PrimeSpectrum.zariskiTopology` supplying the Zariski topology.

annotations.label: Module over a ring

anchor.snippet: A module over a ring is an abelian group that the ring acts on as a ring of endomorphisms

annotations.note: `Module R M` is the standard scalar action of a (semi)ring on an additive commutative group/monoid.

annotations.label: Examples of noncommutative rings

anchor.snippet: Examples of noncommutative rings are given by rings of square matrices

annotations.note: Square matrices form a ring via `Matrix.instRing`, the canonical example of a noncommutative ring.

annotations.label: Representation theory

anchor.snippet: Representation theory is a branch of mathematics that draws heavily on non-commutative rings

annotations.note: A description of a field rather than a formalizable statement, though `Representation`/`Rep` objects exist in Mathlib.RepresentationTheory.

annotations.label: Artin–Wedderburn theorem

anchor.snippet: The Artin–Wedderburn theorem determines the structure of semisimple rings

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Wedderburn–Artin theorem for Artinian simple rings (isomorphic to a matrix ring over a division ring), the building block of the semisimple case.

annotations.label: Jacobson density theorem

anchor.snippet: The Jacobson density theorem determines the structure of primitive rings

annotations.note: `jacobson_density` (with `jacobsonDensity`) is the Jacobson density theorem for modules over their endomorphism ring.

annotations.label: Goldie's theorem

anchor.snippet: Goldie's theorem determines the structure of semiprime Goldie rings

annotations.note: No occurrence of Goldie's theorem or Goldie rings was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zariski–Samuel theorem

anchor.snippet: The Zariski–Samuel theorem determines the structure of a commutative principal ideal ring

annotations.note: No Zariski–Samuel structure theorem for principal ideal rings was found (only bibliographic references to Samuel).

annotations.label: Hopkins–Levitzki theorem

anchor.snippet: The Hopkins–Levitzki theorem gives necessary and sufficient conditions for a Noetherian ring to be an Artinian ring

annotations.note: This is explicitly named as the Hopkins–Levitzki theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Morita theory

anchor.snippet: Morita theory consists of theorems determining when two rings have

annotations.note: Morita equivalence is defined and shown to be an equivalence relation, but the structural Morita-theory characterizations are not formalized.

annotations.label: Cartan–Brauer–Hua theorem

anchor.snippet: Cartan–Brauer–Hua theorem gives insight on the structure of division rings

annotations.note: No Cartan–Brauer–Hua theorem was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wedderburn's little theorem

anchor.snippet: Wedderburn's little theorem states that finite domains are fields

annotations.note: `littleWedderburn` proves every finite division ring is a field.

annotations.label: Skolem–Noether theorem

anchor.snippet: The Skolem–Noether theorem characterizes the automorphisms of simple rings

annotations.note: Skolem–Noether appears only in source comments, not as a proven theorem.

annotations.label: Krull dimension

anchor.snippet: The Krull dimension of R is the supremum of the lengths n of all the chains of prime ideals

annotations.note: `ringKrullDim` is the supremum of lengths of chains of prime ideals.

annotations.label: Dimension of a polynomial ring

anchor.snippet: over a field k has dimension n

annotations.note: Mathlib gives `ringKrullDim (MvPolynomial ι R) = ringKrullDim R + #ι` for Noetherian `R`, which over a field yields dimension `n`.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of dimension theory

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of dimension theory states that the following numbers coincide for a noetherian local ring

annotations.note: Krull's height/principal ideal theorems are formalized, but the full coincidence with Hilbert–Samuel degree and minimal generators is not.

annotations.label: Catenary ring

anchor.snippet: A commutative ring R is said to be catenary

annotations.note: No catenary-ring definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ratliff's catenary criterion

anchor.snippet: Ratliff proved that a noetherian local integral domain R is catenary if and only if for every prime ideal

annotations.note: Neither catenary rings nor Ratliff's criterion appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dimension equals transcendence degree

anchor.snippet: If R is an integral domain that is a finitely generated k -algebra, then its dimension is the transcendence degree of its field of fractions over k

annotations.note: Noether normalization is formalized as the key tool, but the explicit Krull-dimension = transcendence-degree theorem is only flagged as a TODO.

annotations.label: Integral extension preserves dimension

anchor.snippet: If S is an integral extension of a commutative ring R , then S and R have the same dimension

annotations.note: No theorem equating Krull dimensions across an integral extension was found.

annotations.label: Depth bounded by dimension

anchor.snippet: if R is a noetherian local ring, then the depth of R is less than or equal to the dimension of R

annotations.note: The depth of a ring/module is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cohen–Macaulay ring

anchor.snippet: When the equality holds, R is called a Cohen–Macaulay ring

annotations.note: No Cohen–Macaulay ring definition exists in Mathlib (depth is absent).

annotations.label: Regular local ring is Cohen–Macaulay

anchor.snippet: A regular local ring is an example of a Cohen–Macaulay ring

annotations.note: Regular local rings are defined, but Cohen–Macaulay is not, so this implication is not formalized.

annotations.label: Serre's regularity criterion

anchor.snippet: It is a theorem of Serre that R is a regular local ring if and only if it has finite global dimension

annotations.note: `IsRegularLocalRing` is defined but global homological dimension and Serre's criterion are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Morita equivalence

anchor.snippet: Two rings R , S are said to be Morita equivalent if the category of left modules over R is equivalent to the category of left modules over S

annotations.note: `IsMoritaEquivalent` formalizes Morita equivalence via an R-linear module-category equivalence of R-algebras.

annotations.label: Morita equivalent commutative rings are isomorphic

anchor.snippet: two commutative rings which are Morita equivalent must be isomorphic

annotations.note: No theorem stating that Morita-equivalent commutative rings are isomorphic was found.

annotations.label: Picard group

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: It is an abelian group called the Picard group of R

annotations.note: `CommRing.Pic R` is the Picard group of invertible modules over `R`.

annotations.label: Exact sequence for Picard group

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: If R is an integral domain with the field of fractions F of R , then there is an exact sequence of groups

annotations.note: Component maps and quotient isomorphisms (e.g. `unitsToPic`, `unitsQuotEquivRelPic`) exist, but the named units–fractions–Picard exact sequence is not assembled.

annotations.label: Picard group of regular domain

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: If R is a regular domain (i.e., regular at any prime ideal), then Pic(R) is precisely the divisor class group of R

annotations.note: Mathlib relates the class group and Picard group for domains via `ClassGroup.equivPic`, but the regular-domain divisor-class-group statement is not specifically formalized.

annotations.label: Picard group of a PID vanishes

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: if R is a principal ideal domain, then Pic( R ) vanishes

annotations.note: An instance proves `Subsingleton (Pic R)` for any domain with a normalized GCD monoid (including all UFDs and hence PIDs).

annotations.label: Grothendieck group K0(R)

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: this results in a commutative ring K 0 (R)

annotations.note: The algebraic K-theory group K₀ of a ring's finitely generated projective modules is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: K0 invariant under Morita equivalence

anchor.section: Finitely generated projective module over a ring and Picard group
anchor.snippet: Note that K 0 (R) = K 0 (S) if two commutative rings R , S are Morita equivalent

annotations.note: K₀ is not defined, so its Morita invariance cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Simple rings with one-sided ideals

anchor.snippet: there exist simple rings that contain no non-trivial proper (two-sided) ideals, yet contain non-trivial proper left or right ideals

annotations.note: `IsSimpleRing` is defined and matrix rings over simple rings are shown simple, but the existence claim about proper one-sided ideals is not a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Nilradical need not be an ideal

anchor.snippet: the nilradical of a ring , the set of all nilpotent elements, is not necessarily an ideal unless the ring is commutative

annotations.note: `nilradical` is defined only for commutative rings; the noncommutative non-ideal claim is not formalized.

annotations.label: Nilpotent matrices over a division ring

anchor.snippet: the set of all nilpotent elements in the ring of all n × n matrices over a division ring never forms an ideal

annotations.note: No statement that nilpotent matrices over a division ring fail to form an ideal was found.

annotations.label: Jacobson radical

anchor.snippet: the Jacobson radical of a ring; that is, the intersection of all right (left) annihilators of simple right (left) modules over a ring

annotations.note: `Ring.jacobson` is the Jacobson radical, characterized as the infimum of maximal (sub)modules, equivalently annihilators of simple modules.

annotations.label: Equality of maximal one-sided ideal intersections

anchor.snippet: the intersection of all maximal right ideals in a ring is the same as the intersection of all maximal left ideals in the ring

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes the Jacobson radical as an infimum of maximal ideals, but does not explicitly prove the left/right one-sided intersections coincide for noncommutative rings.

annotations.label: Matrix ring is noncommutative

anchor.snippet: the ring of n -by- n matrices over a field is noncommutative

annotations.note: Square matrices over a ring form a ring via `Matrix.instRing`; this is the standard noncommutative example.

annotations.label: Endomorphism ring of the Klein four-group

anchor.snippet: the simplest example being the endomorphism ring of the Klein four-group

annotations.note: The Klein four-group is formalized (`KleinFour`) and `Module.End` exists generically, but its endomorphism ring as a noncommutative example is not constructed.

annotations.label: Quaternions

anchor.snippet: One of the best-known strictly noncommutative ring is the quaternions

annotations.note: `Quaternion R` defines the quaternions, a strictly noncommutative ring/division ring.

annotations.label: Coordinate ring

anchor.section: The coordinate ring of an algebraic variety
anchor.snippet: the set of all regular functions on X forms a ring called the coordinate ring of X

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general coordinate ring of a classical affine variety (only specific cases like elliptic-curve coordinate rings).

annotations.label: Homogeneous coordinate ring

anchor.section: The coordinate ring of an algebraic variety
anchor.snippet: there is an analogous ring called the homogeneous coordinate ring

annotations.note: Graded rings and `ProjectiveSpectrum` exist, but no named homogeneous coordinate ring of a projective variety is defined.

annotations.label: Symmetric polynomials

anchor.snippet: symmetric polynomials are polynomials that are invariant under permutation of variable

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.IsSymmetric` and `symmetricSubalgebra` formalize polynomials invariant under variable permutation.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of symmetric polynomials

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of symmetric polynomials states that this ring is

annotations.note: `esymmAlgEquiv` (with `esymmAlgHom_surjective`/`esymmAlgHom_injective`) gives the algebra isomorphism expressing every symmetric polynomial uniquely in elementary symmetric polynomials.
